source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Anna made the figure on the right out of five cubes. Which of the following figures (when seen from any direction) cannot she get from the figure on the right side if she is allowed to move exactly one cube?
<image1>
<image2>
extraction_notes: options field contains only option labels without option text

issues: metadata.extraction_notes indicates the options field contains only option labels without option text

image_dependency: Question explicitly refers to 'the figure on the right' and asks about resulting figures from moving cubes
Reasoning depends on 3D spatial appearance and views 'from any direction', which cannot be recovered from text alone
Asset registry shows an image exists and registry_passed=true
multi_step: The task requires understanding the initial 5-cube configuration, applying the constraint 'move exactly one cube', and comparing outcomes against multiple candidate figures
The phrase 'when seen from any direction' adds rotational/view-equivalence reasoning beyond a single visual match
There are 5 answer choices, so the solver must eliminate feasible rearrangements and identify the impossible one
This is a spatial transformation problem rather than a direct lookup
verifiability: answer_type is choice/option and answer_text is a single label 'D', giving a clear target output
Question asks for which figure 'cannot' be obtained, so the correctness criterion is well-defined in principle
Verifiability is reduced somewhat because the options field contains only labels without textual descriptions, so checking depends on the image

risk_flags: image-dependent 3D spatial reasoning may be sensitive to visual interpretation
options field contains only option labels without option text

decision_reasons: Core assets are present: question text, primary image, and answer are all available, so the problem is semantically recoverable.
Initial scoring is strong for candidate value, with high image dependency (0.98), multi-step reasoning (0.84), and good verifiability (0.79).
Although the options field lacks option text, the image exists and the task is explicitly image-based, so this is a manageable quality issue rather than a core semantic failure.
Reject is not appropriate because the sample retains sufficient information to support solving and verification.
